lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market contractors in NAICS 238290 that repeatedly install, inspect, maintain, repair, modernize, or rig building and industrial equipment for external customers, including elevators and escalators, commercial doors, conveyors, millwright work, and related equipment services.
lens.excluded_activities: Shells, captive internal maintenance units, non-control financing vehicles, equipment manufacturers without a contracted service operation, and contractors whose work is only a one-off project with no repeat outsourced customer relationship.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial, institutional, industrial, government, and multifamily customers buy project installation or modernization, recurring preventive-maintenance agreements, inspections, emergency callouts, and time-and-materials repairs. Fixed monthly service fees and contract escalators coexist with competitively bid projects and separately billed extra work.

a.bridge: BLS measures the exact industry's occupation counts and wages, not task-level AI substitution. The estimate maps generic language-model exposure evidence onto the wage-weighted NAICS 238290 mix: office support, estimating, project management, sales, dispatch, service records, and troubleshooting support receive higher exposure, while construction, installation, rigging, and physical repair receive low exposure. Generic U.S. workforce exposure tends to overstate substitution in this safety-critical field because it treats affected tasks as technically exposed even when physical execution and licensed judgment remain human; incomplete evidence on field diagnostics could understate the upside.
a.rationale: In 2023, BLS reported that construction and extraction occupations were 46.22% of NAICS 238290 employment and installation, maintenance, and repair occupations were another 21.05%, while office and administrative support was 9.48%, management 6.55%, and business and financial operations 5.09%. The field-heavy mix limits substitution, but document intake, estimating, scheduling, dispatch, invoicing, procurement, customer communications, service-record creation, and technician knowledge retrieval create a material exposed share.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from 20-30 independent NAICS 238290 contractors that allocates payroll by workflow and tests current AI agents on estimating, dispatch, service documentation, parts identification, collections, and diagnostic-assistance tasks would materially move the range.
caveats: The latest directly reachable six-digit BLS occupation table is May 2023 and excludes self-employed workers.
The OpenAI exposure study covers the U.S. workforce broadly and measures technical task exposure, not realized labor removal in this industry.

The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is low quality, uses ancestor 23829 data, and mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts; it does not alter this task-share estimate but weakens later translation into economics.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures current firm use of AI in the broad construction sector, while the AGC survey measures one-year technology investment intentions among $50 million-$500 million contractors. The estimate converts those adoption signals into the five-year share of the exposed NAICS 238290 opportunity that becomes operational after integration, training, quality control, data, and safety constraints. The 2024 BTOS level understates a five-year mature state, while AGC's larger-firm investment intent likely overstates the implementation capacity of lower-middle-market specialists and does not prove labor substitution.
rho.rationale: Construction began from a low AI-use base: Census found 1.4% of construction firms using AI in late 2023 to early 2024. Directionally, adoption intent is much stronger: 54% of surveyed larger contractors expected to increase AI investment in 2025. Over five years, common software-embedded workflows should diffuse, but fragmented systems, weak job-cost data, technician adoption, review requirements, and safety liability keep implementation well below all technically exposed tasks.
rho.change_evidence: A 2028-2031 longitudinal survey of independent equipment contractors, segmented by revenue and service mix, measuring production deployment, active weekly users, cycle-time reductions, error rates, and avoided hires by workflow would materially change this estimate.
caveats: BTOS is sector-wide, not NAICS 238290, and its 2023-2024 question captured use to produce goods and services rather than every internal workflow.
The AGC evidence is investment intent from substantially larger contractors, not implemented benefit among the target firms.
Implementation is likely faster in office workflows than in technician diagnosis or safety-relevant decisions.

e.bridge: Census defines the industry as both installing and servicing diverse building equipment; BLS occupation mix shows substantial repair and maintenance staffing; and Otis reports service as 65% of its global elevator sales. These activity and revenue measures are translated into the share of LMM firms with a recurring or repeat outsourced service relationship. Otis is a global integrated elevator OEM whose service intensity likely overstates eligibility for smaller, project-heavy insulation, rigging, and millwright firms, while a revenue share can differ materially from a firm-count share.
e.rationale: A majority, but not all, target-size firms should qualify. Elevator, mechanical-door, machinery-maintenance, modernization, inspection, and recurring industrial service providers fit directly. Mechanical insulation, one-time installation, dismantling, and project-only rigging businesses may have repeat customers but weaker recurring service characteristics and are excluded when no repeat outsourced relationship exists.
e.change_evidence: A tagged sample of all 191 frozen-band firms using customer concentration, contract mix, repeat-customer revenue, maintenance backlog, and project-only revenue from quality-of-earnings reports or a D&B/Grata/CapIQ company-level screen would materially move eligibility.
caveats: NAICS 238290 is unusually heterogeneous and establishment classifications do not disclose each firm's recurring-revenue mix.
Otis is far larger and more elevator-focused than the target population and includes international revenue.
The frozen firm count is itself an estimate margin-bridged from size-class receipts and a broad Engineering/Construction EBITDA margin; the estimate here does not re-estimate that count.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year intentions among U.S. employer-business owners across industries: 22% planned to sell, transfer, or go public, including 13% planning a sale and 11% a transfer. The estimate uses 22% as the central anchor for a completed qualifying control transfer among eligible LMM firms and widens for intent-to-completion risk and industry differences. Intentions tend to overstate completed transfers, while LMM profitability and demonstrable construction transaction activity may make these firms more transferable than the typical employer business.
s5.rationale: Owner demographics create a real transfer pipeline: Gallup reports 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. Construction businesses also transact in observable numbers, but owner dependence, licensing, bonding, customer relationships, financing, and unsuccessful sale processes prevent every planned exit from becoming a qualifying control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed-deal data for NAICS 238290 from GF Data, DealStats, PitchBook, Axial, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, and specialist brokers, divided by a matched active-firm denominator and adjusted for internal reorganizations, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup measures stated plans rather than completed transactions and is not specific to construction, NAICS 238290, or the EBITDA band.
Gallup respondents could select more than one plan, so component percentages are not additive; the reported 22% summary is used directly.
BizBuySell's construction sample is self-reported and mostly smaller than the $1 million-$10 million EBITDA lens.

q.bridge: A public U.S. elevator-maintenance contract demonstrates equal monthly payments with a 3.5% annual increase and separately billed extra work; KONE reports approximately 90% annual service retention globally; and Otis reports favorable maintenance pricing and productivity alongside inflation. The estimate converts these contract and retention facts into the discounted five-year share of an implemented gross benefit retained by an independent operator. Fixed-fee periods support near-term retention, but renewals, competitive rebids, explicit escalators, customer negotiations, and cost-plus or time-and-materials work pass part of the benefit through. OEM/global retention and a single airport contract likely overstate pricing durability for smaller independents.
q.rationale: AI savings in estimating, dispatch, documentation, billing, and support initially accrue to the contractor when monthly or project prices are fixed. Retention decays through renewals and bids, and transparent time-and-materials work provides less protection. High service continuity and response obligations slow immediate repricing, but customers can demand concessions and competitors can bid against a lower cost base.
q.change_evidence: A contract-level cohort from 20 independent contractors showing pricing basis, escalation clauses, renewal dates, bid win/loss history, gross-margin bridges, and customer concessions before and after documented workflow savings would materially move this value.
caveats: The airport agreement is one large public contract from 2017 and may not represent private or smaller-customer terms.
KONE and Otis are global OEMs with brand, installed-base, and proprietary-equipment advantages that independents may not share.
No source directly observes retention of AI-created gross benefit, so the discount path is judgmental.

d5.bridge: BLS projects elevator-installer and repairer employment to rise 5% from 2024 to 2034 and explicitly attributes sustained demand to regular maintenance, updating old equipment, accessibility, and sophisticated controls. Otis reports 5% organic Service sales growth in 2025, a 4% expansion of its maintenance portfolio to about 2.5 million units, and strong modernization growth. The estimate translates a ten-year U.S. occupation projection and one-year global company revenue/portfolio indicators into a five-year U.S. constant-price, constant-quality quantity ratio for the broader service basket. Employment can understate demand if productivity rises, while nominal pricing and global portfolio acquisitions can overstate U.S. real quantity.
d5.rationale: The installed base requires recurring maintenance, repair, code compliance, emergency response, and modernization even if new nonresidential construction slows. Aging equipment, accessibility needs, and more complex controls offset cyclical exposure in new installation and industrial capital projects, producing modest real quantity growth in the base case.
d5.change_evidence: U.S.-only five-year forecasts for elevator, commercial-door, conveyor, millwright, and mechanical-insulation service units, installed-base age, maintenance-contract counts, modernization backlog, and real service volumes from NEII, NAEC, door associations, and contractor filings would materially move the range.
caveats: The BLS projection covers one important occupation, not the entire heterogeneous NAICS service basket.
Otis is global, elevator-specific, and reports sales and portfolio growth rather than constant-quality U.S. service quantity.
New construction and industrial capital spending can make project portions of demand substantially more cyclical than maintenance.

o.bridge: BLS describes elevator work as on-site assembly, electrical connection, testing, troubleshooting, dismantling, component replacement, preventive maintenance, inspections, and emergency repair; most states require elevator workers to be licensed. The six-digit occupation table also shows that construction and installation/maintenance/repair roles comprise about two-thirds of employment. These work-content measures are translated into the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable contractor. They likely overstate operator need where remote monitoring prevents truck rolls or customers self-perform simple checks, but they omit some regulatory accountability and physical response obligations that support the high end.
o.rationale: Software can triage alerts, predict failures, retrieve technical information, and automate records, but it cannot by itself access shafts and machinery, rig heavy equipment, replace components, validate physical safety, or accept contractual responsibility for emergency response. Most of the current service basket therefore still requires a licensed or accountable operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Fleet-level data comparing connected and unconnected equipment on avoided visits, remote resolution rates, inspection-rule changes, customer self-service, technician hours per maintained unit, and responsibility allocation in service contracts would materially move this estimate.
caveats: Elevator duties and licensing are not representative of every commercial-door, conveyor, insulation, and rigging activity in NAICS 238290.
Rapid remote-diagnostics adoption could reduce routine site quantity faster than current occupational descriptions imply.
The estimate concerns accountable operator requirement, not whether each task remains manually performed.

narrative.executive_view: The opportunity is concentrated in workflow-heavy support around a physical service operation, not in replacing field trades. Repeat maintenance, repair, inspection, modernization, and emergency obligations support durable customer need, while NAICS heterogeneity and uneven recurring-revenue quality require firm-level screening. The frozen labor ratio is low quality and the frozen LMM firm count is estimated, so both should be validated before underwriting.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can compress bid intake, estimating support, scope comparison, scheduling, dispatch, parts lookup, service-record drafting, customer messaging, invoicing, collections, safety-document preparation, and technician knowledge retrieval. Physical installation, inspection, troubleshooting, rigging, component replacement, and final safety accountability remain bottlenecks, so gains are more likely to appear as avoided office hiring and higher technician utilization than wholesale technician substitution.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed monthly maintenance fees, annual escalators, project pricing, and separately billed extra work allow some benefit to remain with the operator before renewal. Capture erodes as contracts reprice, customers negotiate, and competitors bid from similar tools; transparent hourly and cost-plus work is especially susceptible to sharing. Contract duration, proprietary-equipment access, response performance, and customer retention are therefore central diligence items.
narrative.firm_availability: Employer-owner succession evidence points to meaningful five-year transfer intent, and construction businesses are visibly bought and sold. However, a qualifying transfer depends on a transferable technician bench, licenses, bonding, documented customer contracts, vendor and OEM access, and low owner dependence. Project-only insulation and rigging shops should not be treated like maintenance-route businesses merely because they share the NAICS code.
narrative.demand_durability: Regular maintenance, code compliance, emergency response, equipment wear, accessibility needs, and modernization of aging controls sustain the core service basket. New-installation and industrial-project work remain cyclical, but the installed base and physical safety obligations make full software displacement unlikely within five years.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainty is mix: NAICS 238290 combines attractive contracted service with one-off installation, insulation, dismantling, and rigging. Other risks include OEM proprietary systems, union or licensed-labor constraints, technician scarcity, customer concentration, public bidding, weak field data, cybersecurity of connected equipment, safety liability, and savings being competed away. Firm-level contract and workflow evidence could move the primitives materially in either direction.
narrative.principal_driver: A safety-critical installed equipment base creates repeat workflows where AI can increase office and technician throughput without eliminating the accountable field operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: The broad NAICS code obscures how much of the LMM firm population actually has transferable recurring service revenue rather than cyclical project work.

sources.title: NAICS 238290 Other Building Equipment Contractors definition
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments installing or servicing building equipment and expressly includes maintenance and repairs, while listing heterogeneous activities such as elevators, commercial doors, conveyors, insulation, machine rigging, and millwright work.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Estimates: NAICS 238290
sources.supports: Reports 153,170 jobs and the exact industry occupation mix and wages, including 46.22% construction and extraction, 21.05% installation/maintenance/repair, 9.48% office support, 6.55% management, and 5.09% business and financial operations.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Elevator and Escalator Installers and Repairers
sources.supports: Describes physical, technical, recordkeeping, preventive-maintenance, inspection, and emergency-repair duties; states most states require licensing; and projects 5% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, supported by maintenance, updating old equipment, accessibility, and sophisticated controls.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An early look at the labor market impact potential of large language models
sources.supports: Provides broad U.S. task-exposure evidence: about 80% of workers could have at least 10% of tasks affected and about 19% could have at least 50% affected, used only as a cross-occupation technical-exposure proxy.

sources.title: Tracking Firm Use of AI in Real Time: A Snapshot from the Business Trends and Outlook Survey
sources.supports: Reports nationally representative business AI use rising from 3.7% to 5.4% from September 2023 to February 2024 and construction at 1.4%, with only 1.9% of construction workers at firms using AI.

sources.title: AGC 2025 Construction Outlook: Firms with $50 Million to $500 Million in Revenue
sources.supports: Among 325 respondents, 54% expected increased AI investment in 2025, alongside planned increases in document management, project management, accounting, estimating, CRM, scheduling, and service-management software.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer ownership, or go public within five years.

sources.title: Otis Worldwide Corporation 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports Service at 65% of 2025 sales and 91% of segment profit; approximately 2.5 million units in the global maintenance portfolio; 5% Service organic sales growth; maintenance pricing and productivity benefits; and 2025 revenue split among new equipment, maintenance and repair, and modernization.

sources.title: Hillsborough County Aviation Authority Elevator, Escalator, Moving Walkway and Dumbwaiter Maintenance Contract
sources.supports: Shows a U.S. public maintenance contract paid in equal monthly installments, adjusted by a 3.5% annual price increase, with extra work and emergency callbacks billed under separate labor rates and response obligations.

sources.title: KONE as an investment
sources.supports: States that Service provides a stable recurring revenue base driven by safety, reliability, and long customer relationships, with approximately 90% annual retention; also distinguishes more cyclical new-building and modernization work.

sources.title: Construction Business Valuation Multiples and Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Reports 3,142 sold construction listings analyzed for 2021-2025, median 207 days on market, median $750,000 sale price, $1.51 million revenue, $323,174 owner earnings, and stable transaction multiples, demonstrating an observable but smaller-company construction transfer market.
